title: Nuclear fission and fusion
examTip: Fission and fusion both release energy by a change in total nuclear mass — but they work in opposite directions. Fission: large nucleus splits into two smaller ones. Fusion: two small nuclei combine into a larger one. Know which fuels are used for each (U-235/Pu-239 for fission; hydrogen isotopes for fusion).
introTips: Nuclear reactions release millions of times more energy per reaction than chemical reactions like burning — that is why a tiny amount of fuel produces so much power.
Fission is what happens inside every nuclear power station today — it is proven technology.
Fusion is what powers the Sun and all stars — but we have not yet managed to sustain it for long enough or cheaply enough to generate electricity commercially.
In a chain reaction, each fission releases neutrons that can trigger further fissions — without control, this becomes an explosion (atomic bomb); controlled, it powers a reactor.
Nuclear power produces no carbon dioxide during operation — but radioactive waste must be stored safely for thousands of years.
intro: When Einstein wrote E = mc², he hinted at something astonishing: mass and energy are two forms of the same thing, and you can convert between them. Nuclear reactions do exactly this — a tiny loss of mass produces an enormous release of energy. Fission powers nuclear submarines, aircraft carriers, and about 10% of the world's electricity. Fusion powers every star in the universe, releasing energy that has made life on Earth possible. Scientists have been trying to harness fusion since the 1950s — if they succeed, it could provide virtually limitless clean energy. It is one of the biggest engineering challenges in history.

keyPoints.heading: Nuclear fission
keyPoints.content: Nuclear fission is the process by which a large unstable nucleus absorbs a neutron and splits into two smaller nuclei (called fission fragments), releasing 2 or 3 neutrons and a large amount of energy (as kinetic energy of the fragments and gamma radiation). The most common fuels are uranium-235 (U-235) and plutonium-239 (Pu-239). These nuclei are large enough to be unstable after absorbing a neutron, causing them to split. The energy released per fission reaction is approximately 200 MeV — compared to a few eV for a chemical reaction. This enormous energy difference (about 50 million times greater) is why such small amounts of nuclear fuel produce so much energy. The two smaller nuclei produced (fission fragments) are themselves radioactive and must be managed as nuclear waste.
writeBullets: Fission: large nucleus absorbs a neutron → splits into two smaller nuclei + 2–3 neutrons + energy
Fuels: uranium-235 (U-235) or plutonium-239 (Pu-239)
Releases ~200 MeV per reaction — millions of times more energy than a chemical reaction
Fission fragments are radioactive — must be stored as nuclear waste
Energy released as kinetic energy of fragments and gamma radiation

keyTerms.def: A large nucleus absorbs a neutron and splits into two smaller nuclei, releasing 2–3 neutrons and a large amount of energy
One of the two smaller nuclei produced when a large nucleus undergoes fission; typically radioactive
A fissile isotope used as fuel in nuclear reactors; splits when it absorbs a slow neutron

keyPoints.memTip: Fission = split. Large nucleus + neutron → two smaller nuclei + 2–3 neutrons + enormous energy.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Mabel should be able to describe the process step by step: neutron absorbed → nucleus splits → new nuclei + more neutrons + energy — in that order.

keyPoints.heading: Chain reactions and controlling fission
keyPoints.content: Each fission releases 2 or 3 neutrons. If these neutrons go on to trigger further fissions, which each release more neutrons, you get a chain reaction. Uncontrolled chain reaction: each fission triggers multiple further fissions exponentially → explosive release of energy → nuclear weapon (atomic bomb). Controlled chain reaction: the rate of reaction is kept steady — one neutron from each fission goes on to trigger exactly one more fission. This is achieved using: Control rods (absorb excess neutrons — pushed in to slow down, pulled out to speed up; made of boron or cadmium). Moderator (slows neutrons down to the right speed for U-235 to absorb — usually water or graphite). In a nuclear power station, the heat from fission is used to produce steam, which drives turbines and generates electricity — just like a conventional power station but using nuclear fuel instead of burning fossil fuels.
writeBullets: Chain reaction: neutrons from each fission trigger further fissions → self-sustaining process
Uncontrolled: exponential growth → explosion (nuclear weapon)
Controlled: each fission triggers exactly one more → steady energy release in a reactor
Control rods: absorb neutrons; pushed in slows reaction, pulled out speeds it up (boron or cadmium)
Moderator: slows neutrons to speed absorbed by U-235 (usually water or graphite)

keyTerms.def: Each fission event releases neutrons that trigger further fissions — self-sustaining if controlled, explosive if uncontrolled
Rods of boron or cadmium inserted into a reactor to absorb neutrons and control the rate of fission
Material (water or graphite) that slows neutrons to the correct speed for absorption by U-235

keyPoints.memTip: Control rods in → fewer neutrons → reaction slows. Control rods out → more neutrons → reaction speeds up.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Mabel should be able to draw/describe the chain reaction diagram: one fission → 2–3 neutrons → more fissions — showing why it multiplies.

keyPoints.heading: Nuclear fusion
keyPoints.content: Nuclear fusion is the process by which two small nuclei combine to form a larger nucleus, releasing large amounts of energy. The most studied reaction for energy generation uses deuterium (²H) and tritium (³H) — two isotopes of hydrogen — combining to form helium plus a neutron plus energy: ²₁H + ³₁H → ⁴₂He + ¹₀n + energy. Fusion releases even more energy per kilogram of fuel than fission. Fusion is the energy source of stars (including the Sun) — in the Sun's core, hydrogen nuclei fuse to form helium under enormous gravitational pressure and temperatures of ~15 million °C. Advantages over fission: more energy per kg, fuel (hydrogen) is abundant, no long-lived radioactive waste. Challenge: requires extremely high temperatures (~100 million °C on Earth) and pressures to overcome the electrostatic repulsion between positively charged nuclei — sustaining these conditions long enough to produce net energy has not yet been achieved at commercial scale.
writeBullets: Fusion: two small nuclei combine to form a larger nucleus + energy
Typical reaction: deuterium + tritium → helium + neutron + energy
Powers all stars including the Sun — occurs at millions of °C and extreme pressure
Releases more energy per kg than fission; fuel is abundant; no long-lived waste
Not yet commercially viable — requires ~100 million °C to overcome electrostatic repulsion on Earth

keyTerms.def: Two small nuclei combine to form a larger nucleus, releasing energy — the process that powers stars
Hydrogen isotopes used as fuel in fusion reactions; abundant and available from seawater (deuterium)
The force between two positively charged nuclei that must be overcome for fusion to occur — requires very high temperatures

keyPoints.memTip: Fusion = join. Small + small → bigger + energy. Powers the Sun. Needs extreme heat to overcome repulsion between positive nuclei.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Mabel should be clear on WHY fusion needs such high temperatures: to give nuclei enough kinetic energy to overcome the electrostatic repulsion and get close enough to fuse.

keyPoints.heading: Comparing fission and fusion — nuclear power evaluation
keyPoints.content: Fission advantages: proven technology, large-scale electricity generation, no CO₂ emissions during operation, reliable (not weather-dependent). Fission disadvantages: radioactive waste remains hazardous for thousands of years (requires secure storage), risk of accidents (Chernobyl, Fukushima), high construction cost, uranium fuel will eventually run out (not truly renewable), risk of nuclear proliferation (waste can be used for weapons). Fusion advantages: more energy per kg, fuel (hydrogen from seawater) is effectively limitless, no long-lived radioactive waste, no CO₂, no meltdown risk. Fusion disadvantages: requires conditions not yet achievable at commercial scale (100 million °C), huge engineering challenge, has required enormous investment for decades with no commercial output yet — 'always 30 years away'. Both processes release energy due to a small change in nuclear mass — this mass difference is converted to energy according to E = mc².
writeBullets: Fission: no CO₂, reliable, proven — but long-lived radioactive waste, accident risk, limited fuel
Fusion: limitless fuel, no long-lived waste, more energy per kg — but not yet commercially viable
Both: no CO₂ emissions during operation; energy from mass change (E = mc²)
Fusion challenge: 100 million °C needed to overcome electrostatic repulsion between nuclei
Nuclear waste from fission must be stored safely for thousands of years — major political/engineering challenge

keyTerms.def: Radioactive fission fragments and other materials from nuclear reactors — remain hazardous for thousands of years
Einstein's equation showing the relationship between mass and energy — a tiny loss of mass produces enormous energy in nuclear reactions

keyPoints.memTip: Fission = proven but waste problem. Fusion = ideal but not yet working commercially. Both: no CO₂ during operation.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Evaluation questions about nuclear power are common 6-mark questions — Mabel should practise writing a balanced argument covering advantages and disadvantages of fission, then comparing with fusion.

tables.title: Fission vs fusion comparison
rows: Large nucleus splits into two smaller ones
Two small nuclei combine into a larger one
~17.6 MeV per D-T reaction, but more per kg of fuel
Commercial power stations in use globally
Short-lived or none — much smaller problem

0.question: In nuclear fission, what triggers a uranium-235 nucleus to split?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! A slow neutron is absorbed by the U-235 nucleus, making it unstable enough to split into two smaller nuclei and release 2–3 more neutrons.
0.wrongFeedback: Fission is triggered when a U-235 nucleus absorbs a slow neutron — this makes it unstable and it splits, releasing more neutrons and a large amount of energy.

1.question: What is the role of control rods in a nuclear reactor?
options: They absorb neutrons to control the rate of fission
They slow neutrons down to the speed needed for absorption by U-235
They cool the reactor by absorbing heat from the fuel
They shield the reactor from radiation
1.correctFeedback: Correct! Control rods (made of boron or cadmium) absorb neutrons. Push them in further → fewer neutrons available → reaction slows. Pull them out → more neutrons → reaction speeds up.
1.wrongFeedback: Control rods absorb neutrons — this is how they control the reaction rate. The moderator (water or graphite) is what slows the neutrons down. These are two different components with different jobs.

3.question: Why is nuclear fusion not currently used to generate electricity on a large scale?
options: It requires temperatures of around 100 million °C which are extremely difficult to sustain, and has not yet produced net energy output at commercial scale
The fuel (uranium) needed for fusion is very rare and expensive
Fusion produces more radioactive waste than fission
Fusion reactions are too small to produce useful amounts of energy
3.correctFeedback: Correct! Fusion requires extreme temperatures (~100 million °C on Earth) to overcome electrostatic repulsion between positive nuclei. Sustaining this long enough to produce net energy has not yet been achieved commercially.
3.wrongFeedback: Fusion uses hydrogen isotopes (not uranium) as fuel — they are abundant. The problem is the extreme conditions needed: ~100 million °C to overcome electrostatic repulsion. This has not yet been sustained long enough for commercial energy generation.

commonMistakes: Confusing fission and fusion — fission splits a large nucleus; fusion joins two small ones. A useful memory: Fission = Fragment (split apart); Fusion = Fuse (join together).
Saying the moderator controls the rate of the reaction — the moderator slows neutrons to the right speed; the control rods control the reaction rate by absorbing neutrons.
Thinking fusion produces lots of radioactive waste like fission — fusion's main product is helium, with much shorter-lived radioactivity than fission fragments.
Forgetting that fission releases 2 or 3 neutrons (not just 1) — this is what makes the chain reaction possible.
Stating that nuclear power produces CO₂ — it does not during operation. It has a carbon footprint from construction and fuel processing, but not from generating electricity.
examTips: For chain reaction questions: explain the process step by step — neutron absorbed by U-235 → splits → releases 2–3 neutrons → these trigger further fissions → chain reaction.
Control rods vs moderator: control rods absorb neutrons (controlling rate); moderator slows neutrons (making them absorbable). Know both and do not mix them up.
In evaluation questions about nuclear power, give both advantages and disadvantages — a one-sided answer will not score full marks on a 6-marker.

For fusion questions: always explain WHY high temperature is needed — to give nuclei enough kinetic energy to overcome the electrostatic repulsion between positive charges and get close enough to fuse.
revisionCardBullets: Fission: large nucleus + neutron → 2 smaller nuclei + 2–3 neutrons + energy
Chain reaction: each fission triggers more — controlled (reactor) or uncontrolled (bomb)
Control rods: absorb neutrons, control reaction rate (boron or cadmium)
Moderator: slows neutrons for absorption by U-235 (water or graphite)
Fusion: two small nuclei (e.g. deuterium + tritium) combine → larger nucleus + energy
Fusion powers the Sun and all stars
Fusion needs ~100 million °C to overcome electrostatic repulsion
Fusion: more energy per kg, abundant fuel, no long-lived waste — but not yet commercially viable
Fission: no CO₂ during operation — but long-lived radioactive waste is a major challenge
Both fission and fusion: energy comes from a tiny change in nuclear mass (E = mc²)
